lt.gd: This bill transfers oversight of public school construction from the State Board of Education to the Division of Facilities Construction and Management (division).
lt.hp: This bill:establishes permitting and inspection authority for the division regarding public school construction projects;repeals and reenacts sections related to state board coordination with the division;establishes educational facility planning requirements for a school district including notification requirements;establishes public school construction oversight under the division;establishes division authority to adopt comprehensive construction rules covering square footage, safety, costs, and building standards;requires consultation with the state security chief on mandatory safety and security standards;creates architect and design professional cost oversight with benchmarks and review requirements;establishes plan review and approval authority for the division before construction can begin;sets mandatory technical compliance requirements including fire safety, accessibility, and energy codes;creates an online document management system for construction documentation and compliance tracking;establishes inspection verification and monitoring procedures with qualified inspector requirements;implements standardized cost reporting systems with detailed requirements and cost database development;requires a school district to obtain division approval that projects fit within established cost matrices before construction;requires training and technical assistance programs including resource manuals and annual training conferences;authorizes the division to delegate oversight authority to qualified school districts with demonstrated internal capacity; authorizes the division to establish a fee schedule to recover costs of oversight activities;designates a school construction liaison for coordination between the state board and division;allows division-managed construction services for a school district with fee structures and reimbursement requirements;creates enforcement and penalty provisions including administrative penalties up to $25,000 and mandatory compliance audits;establishes a qualified contractor and vendor registry for public school construction projects;requires a school district to submit historical project information and documentation to the division upon request;authorizes the division to establish a fee schedule to recover costs of oversight activities;provides the division with construction permitting authority;sets an implementation timeline requiring full compliance by January 1, 2027; andmakes conforming changes.
lt.oc: Other Special Clauses:This bill provides a special effective date.
lt.sa: Utah Code Sections Affected:AMENDS:10-20-304, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2025, First Special Session, Chapter 1517-79-305, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2025, First Special Session, Chapter 1463G-6a-1302, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2022, Chapter 421ENACTS:63A-5b-1201, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1202, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1203, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1204, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1205, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1206, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1207, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1208, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1209, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1210, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1211, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1212, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1213, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1214, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1215, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1216, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1217, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1218, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1219, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1220, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1221, Utah Code Annotated 195363A-5b-1222, Utah Code Annotated 1953REPEALS AND REENACTS:53E-3-701, as enacted by Laws of Utah 2018, Chapter 153E-3-702, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2024, Chapter 2153E-3-703, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2019, Chapter 18653E-3-704, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2018, Chapter 1REPEALS:53E-3-705, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2024, Chapter 8553E-3-706, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2024, Chapter 2153E-3-707, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2019, Chapter 18653E-3-708, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2018, Chapter 153E-3-709, as last amended by Laws of Utah 2019, Chapter 18653E-3-710, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2018, Chapter 153E-3-711, as renumbered and amended by Laws of Utah 2018, Chapter 1

leg.enact: Be it enacted by the Legislature of the state of Utah:

section.secline: Section 1. Section 10-20-304 is amended to read:

catline.bold: 10-20-304. Political subdivisions required to conform to municipality's land use ordinances -- Exceptions.

subsection.subsection: (a)Each county, municipality, school district, charter school, special district, special service district, and political subdivision of the state shall conform to any applicable land use ordinance of any municipality when installing, constructing, operating, or otherwise using any area, land, or building situated within that municipality.

(b)In addition to any other remedies provided by law, when a municipality's land use ordinance is violated or about to be violated by another political subdivision, that municipality may institute an injunction, mandamus, abatement, or other appropriate action or proceeding to prevent, enjoin, abate, or remove the improper installation, improvement, or use.
(a)Except as provided in Subsection (3), a school district or charter school is subject to a municipality's land use ordinances.

(i)Notwithstanding Subsection (3), a municipality may:(A)subject a charter school to standards within each zone pertaining to setback, height, bulk and massing regulations, off-site parking, curb cut, traffic circulation, and construction staging; and(B)impose regulations upon the location of a project that are necessary to avoid unreasonable risks to health or safety, as provided in Subsection (3)(f).
(ii)The standards to which a municipality may subject a charter school under Subsection (2)(b)(i) shall be objective standards only and may not be subjective.

(iii)Except as provided in Subsection (7)(d), the only basis upon which a municipality may deny or withhold approval of a charter school's land use application is the charter school's failure to comply with a standard imposed under Subsection (2)(b)(i).

(iv)Nothing in Subsection (2)(b)(iii) may be construed to relieve a charter school of an obligation to comply with a requirement of an applicable building or safety code to which it is otherwise obligated to comply.

section.subsection: (3)A municipality may not:(a)impose requirements for landscaping, fencing, aesthetic considerations, construction methods or materials, additional building inspections, municipal building codes, building use for educational purposes, or the placement or use of temporary classroom facilities on school property;(b)except as otherwise provided in this section, require a school district or charter school to participate in the cost of any roadway or sidewalk, or a study on the impact of a school on a roadway or sidewalk, that is not reasonably necessary for the safety of school children and not located on or contiguous to school property, unless the roadway or sidewalk is required to connect an otherwise isolated school site to an existing roadway;(c)require a district or charter school to pay fees not authorized by this section;(d)provide for inspection of school construction or assess a fee or other charges for inspection, unless the school district or charter school is unable to provide for inspection by an inspector, other than the project architect or contractor, who is qualified under criteria established by the state superintendent;(e)require a school district or charter school to pay any impact fee for an improvement project unless the impact fee is imposed as provided in Title 11, Chapter 36a, Impact Fees Act;(f)impose regulations upon the location of an educational facility except as necessary to avoid unreasonable risks to health or safety; or(g)for a land use or a structure owned or operated by a school district or charter school that is not an educational facility but is used in support of providing instruction to pupils, impose a regulation that:(i)is not imposed on a similar land use or structure in the zone in which the land use or structure is approved; or(ii)uses the tax exempt status of the school district or charter school as criteria for prohibiting or regulating the land use or location of the structure.

(4)Subject to Section 53E-3-71053E-3-703 and in accordance with standards the Division of Facilities Construction and Management establishes in rule, a school district or charter school shall coordinate the siting of a new school with the municipality in which the school is to be located, to:(a)avoid or mitigate existing and potential traffic hazards, including consideration of the impacts between the new school and future highways; and(b)maximize school, student, and site safety.

(5)Notwithstanding Subsection (3)(d), a municipality may, at itsthe municipality's discretion:(a)providerequest a walk-through of school construction at no cost and at a time convenient to the school district or charter school; and(b)provide recommendations based upon the walk-through. to:(i)the school district or charter school; and(ii)the Division of Facilities Construction and Management.

subsection.amend: The Division of Facilities Construction and Management has the sole authority to approve inspectors for school construction projects under Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight.
A school district shall use only inspectors approved by the Division of Facilities Construction and Management as follows:
a Division of Facilities Construction and Management inspector;
a municipal building inspector who is on the division's approved roster under Section 63A-5b-1220; or
a certified building inspector who meets all of the following requirements:
is on the Division of Facilities Construction and Management's approved roster under Section 63A-5b-1220;
is not an employee of the contractor;
is licensed to perform the inspection requested; and
maintains current certifications as required by Division of Facilities Construction and Management's rule.
Notwithstanding Subsection (3)(d), a school district or charter school shall use:
a municipal building inspector;
for a school district, a school district building inspector from that school district; or
for a charter school, a school district building inspector from the school district in which the charter school is located; or

an independent, certified building inspector who is not an employee of the contractor, licensed to perform the inspection that the inspector is requested to perform, and approved by a municipal building inspector or:
for a school district, a school district building inspector from that school district; or
for a charter school, a school district building inspector from the school district in which the charter school is located.
The approval under Subsection (6)(a)(iii) may not be unreasonably withheld.

If a school district or charter school uses a school district or independent building inspector under Subsection (6)(a)(ii) or (iii), the school district or charter school shall submit to the state superintendent of public instruction and municipal building official, on a monthly basis during construction of the school building, a copy of each inspection certificate regarding the school building.

subsection.subsection: (a)A charter school, home-based microschool, or micro-education entity shall be considered a permitted use in all zoning districts within a municipality.

(b)Each land use application for any approval required for a charter school, home-based microschool, or micro-education entity, including an application for a building permit, shall be processed on a first priority basis.
(c)Parking requirements for a charter school or a micro-education entity may not exceed the minimum parking requirements for schools or other institutional public uses throughout the municipality.

(d)If a municipality has designated zones for a sexually oriented business, or a business which sells alcohol, a charter school or a micro-education entity may be prohibited from a location which would otherwise defeat the purpose for the zone unless the charter school or micro-education entity provides a waiver.

subsection.amend: A certificate authorizing permanent occupancy issued by the Division of Facilities Construction and Management under Section 63A-5b-1208 shall be the exclusive certificate required, and the municipality shall accept the Division of Facilities Construction and Management's certificate as satisfying all local occupancy requirements.

A micro-education entity is not subject to the requirements of Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight, and shall comply with local building codes and permitting requirements through municipal or county building officials.
A school district, charter school, or micro-education entity may seek a certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building from:

the state superintendent of public instruction, as provided in Subsection 53E-3-706(3), if the school district or charter school used an independent building inspector for inspection of the school building; or

a municipal official with authority to issue the certificate, if the school district, charter school, or micro-education entity used a municipal building inspector for inspection of the school building.

A school district may issue its own certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building if it used its own building inspector for inspection of the school building, subject to the notification requirement of Subsection 53E-3-706(3)(a).

A charter school or micro-education entity may seek a certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building from a school district official with authority to issue the certificate, if the charter school or micro-education entity used a school district building inspector for inspection of the school building.

A certificate authorizing permanent occupancy issued by the state superintendent of public instruction under Subsection 53E-3-706(3) or a school district official with authority to issue the certificate shall be considered to satisfy any municipal requirement for an inspection or a certificate of occupancy.

subsection.subsection: (i)A micro-education entity may operate in a facility that meets Group E Occupancy requirements as defined by the International Building Code, as incorporated by Section 15A-2-103.
(ii)A micro-education entity operating in a facility described in Subsection (7)(f)(i) may have up to 100 students in the facility.

(g)A micro-education entity may operate in a facility that is subject to and complies with the same occupancy requirements as a Class A-1, A-3, B, or M Occupancy as defined by the International Building Code, as incorporated by Section 15A-2-103, if:(i)the facility has a code compliant fire alarm system and carbon monoxide detection system;(ii)(A)each classroom in the facility has an exit directly to the outside at the level of exit or discharge; or(B)the structure has a code compliant fire sprinkler system; and(iii)the facility has an automatic fire sprinkler system in fire areas of the facility that are greater than 12,000 square feet.
(i)A home-based microschool is not subject to additional occupancy requirements beyond occupancy requirements that apply to a primary dwelling.

(ii)If a floor that is below grade in a home-based microschool is used for home-based microschool purposes, the below grade floor of the home-based microschool shall have at least one emergency escape or rescue window that complies with the requirements for emergency escape and rescue windows as defined by the International Residential Code, as incorporated by Section 15A-2-103.

(a)A specified public agency intending to develop its land shall submit to the land use authority a development plan and schedule:(i)as early as practicable in the development process, but no later than the commencement of construction; and(ii)with sufficient detail to enable the land use authority to assess:(A)the specified public agency's compliance with applicable land use ordinances;(B)the demand for public facilities listed in Subsections 11-36a-102(17)(a), (b), (c), (d), (e), and (g) caused by the development;(C)the amount of any applicable fee described in Sections 10-20-904 and 10-20-910;(D)any credit against an impact fee; and(E)the potential for waiving an impact fee.

(b)The land use authority shall respond to a specified public agency's submission under Subsection (8)(a) with reasonable promptness in order to allow the specified public agency to consider information the municipality provides under Subsection (8)(a)(ii) in the process of preparing the budget for the development.

section.subsection: (9)Nothing in this section may be construed to:(a)modify or supersede Section 10-20-305; or(b)authorize a municipality to enforce an ordinance in a way, or enact an ordinance, that fails to comply with Title 57, Chapter 21, Utah Fair Housing Act, the federal Fair Housing Amendments Act of 1988, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 3601 et seq., the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 12102, or any other provision of federal law.

(10)Nothing in Subsection (7) prevents a political subdivision from:(a)requiring a home-based microschool or micro-education entity to comply with municipal zoning and land use regulations that do not conflict with this section, including:(i)parking;(ii)traffic; and(iii)hours of operation;(b)requiring a home-based microschool or micro-education entity to obtain a business license;(c)enacting municipal ordinances and regulations consistent with this section;(d)subjecting a micro-education entity to standards within each zone pertaining to setback, height, bulk and massing regulations, off-site parking, curb cut, traffic circulation, and construction staging; and(e)imposing regulations on the location of a project that are necessary to avoid risks to health or safety.

subsection.amend: Section 63A-5b-1218 governs the requirements for permitting and inspection of public school buildings.
A municipality may not enact any ordinance, policy, or regulation relating to the permitting or inspection of public school buildings.
Nothing in this section shall prohibit the required regulation or subsequent inspection by a local health department.

section.secline: Section 2. Section 17-79-305 is amended to read:

catline.bold: 17-79-305. Other entities required to conform to county's land use ordinances -- Exceptions -- School districts, charter schools, home-based microschools, and micro-education entities -- Submission of development plan and schedule.

subsection.subsection: (a)Each county, municipality, school district, charter school, special district, special service district, and political subdivision of the state shall conform to any applicable land use ordinance of any county when installing, constructing, operating, or otherwise using any area, land, or building situated within a mountainous planning district or the unincorporated portion of the county, as applicable.

(b)In addition to any other remedies provided by law, when a county's land use ordinance is violated or about to be violated by another political subdivision, that county may institute an injunction, mandamus, abatement, or other appropriate action or proceeding to prevent, enjoin, abate, or remove the improper installation, improvement, or use.
(a)Except as provided in Subsection (3), a school district or charter school is subject to a county's land use ordinances.

(i)Notwithstanding Subsection (3), a county may:(A)subject a charter school to standards within each zone pertaining to setback, height, bulk and massing regulations, off-site parking, curb cut, traffic circulation, and construction staging; and(B)impose regulations upon the location of a project that are necessary to avoid unreasonable risks to health or safety, as provided in Subsection (3)(f).
(ii)The standards to which a county may subject a charter school under Subsection (2)(b)(i) shall be objective standards only and may not be subjective.

(iii)Except as provided in Subsection (7)(d), the only basis upon which a county may deny or withhold approval of a charter school's land use application is the charter school's failure to comply with a standard imposed under Subsection (2)(b)(i).

(iv)Nothing in Subsection (2)(b)(iii) may be construed to relieve a charter school of an obligation to comply with a requirement of an applicable building or safety code to which it is otherwise obligated to comply.

section.subsection: (3)A county may not:(a)impose requirements for landscaping, fencing, aesthetic considerations, construction methods or materials, additional building inspections, county building codes, building use for educational purposes, or the placement or use of temporary classroom facilities on school property;(b)except as otherwise provided in this section, require a school district or charter school to participate in the cost of any roadway or sidewalk, or a study on the impact of a school on a roadway or sidewalk, that is not reasonably necessary for the safety of school children and not located on or contiguous to school property, unless the roadway or sidewalk is required to connect an otherwise isolated school site to an existing roadway;(c)require a district or charter school to pay fees not authorized by this section;(d)provide for inspection of school construction or assess a fee or other charges for inspection, unless the school district or charter school is unable to provide for inspection by an inspector, other than the project architect or contractor, who is qualified under criteria established by the state superintendent;(e)require a school district or charter school to pay any impact fee for an improvement project unless the impact fee is imposed as provided in Title 11, Chapter 36a, Impact Fees Act;(f)impose regulations upon the location of an educational facility except as necessary to avoid unreasonable risks to health or safety; or(g)for a land use or a structure owned or operated by a school district or charter school that is not an educational facility but is used in support of providing instruction to pupils, impose a regulation that:(i)is not imposed on a similar land use or structure in the zone in which the land use or structure is approved; or(ii)uses the tax exempt status of the school district or charter school as criteria for prohibiting or regulating the land use or location of the structure.

(4)Subject to Section 53E-3-71053E-3-703 and in accordance with standards the Division of Facilities Construction and Management establishes in rule, a school district or charter school shall coordinate the siting of a new school with the county in which the school is to be located, to:(a)avoid or mitigate existing and potential traffic hazards, including consideration of the impacts between the new school and future highways; and(b)maximize school, student, and site safety.

(5)Notwithstanding Subsection (3)(d), a county may, at itsthe county's discretion:(a)providerequest a walk-through of school construction at no cost and at a time convenient to the school district or charter school; and(b)provide recommendations based upon the walk-through. to:(i)the school district or charter school; and(ii)the Division of Facilities Construction and Management.

subsection.amend: The Division of Facilities Construction and Management has the sole authority to approve inspectors for school construction projects under Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight.
A school district shall use only inspectors approved by the Division of Facilities Construction and Management as follows:
a Division of Facilities Construction and Management inspector;
a county building inspector who is on the Division of Facilities Construction and Management's approved roster under Section 63A-5b-1220; or
a certified building inspector who meets all of the following requirements:
is on the Division of Facilities Construction and Management's approved roster under Section 63A-5b-1220;
is not an employee of the contractor;
is licensed to perform the inspection requested; and
maintains current certifications as required by Division of Facilities Construction and Management rule.
Notwithstanding Subsection (3)(d), a school district or charter school shall use:
a county building inspector;
for a school district, a school district building inspector from that school district; or
for a charter school, a school district building inspector from the school district in which the charter school is located; or

an independent, certified building inspector who is not an employee of the contractor, licensed to perform the inspection that the inspector is requested to perform, and approved by a county building inspector or:
for a school district, a school district building inspector from that school district; or
for a charter school, a school district building inspector from the school district in which the charter school is located.
The approval under Subsection (6)(a)(iii) may not be unreasonably withheld.

If a school district or charter school uses a school district or independent building inspector under Subsection (6)(a)(ii) or (iii), the school district or charter school shall submit to the state superintendent of public instruction and county building official, on a monthly basis during construction of the school building, a copy of each inspection certificate regarding the school building.

subsection.subsection: (a)A charter school, home-based microschool, or micro-education entity shall be considered a permitted use in all zoning districts within a county.

(b)Each land use application for any approval required for a charter school, home-based microschool, or micro-education entity, including an application for a building permit, shall be processed on a first priority basis.
(c)Parking requirements for a charter school or micro-education entity may not exceed the minimum parking requirements for schools or other institutional public uses throughout the county.

(d)If a county has designated zones for a sexually oriented business, or a business which sells alcohol, a charter school or micro-education entity may be prohibited from a location which would otherwise defeat the purpose for the zone unless the charter school or micro-education entity provides a waiver.

subsection.amend: A certificate authorizing permanent occupancy issued by the Division of Facilities Construction and Management under Section 63A-5b-1208 shall be the exclusive certificate required, and a county shall accept the division's certificate as satisfying all local occupancy requirements.

A micro-education entity is not subject to the requirements of Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight, and shall comply with local building codes and permitting requirements through county building officials.
A school district , charter school, or micro-education entity may seek a certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building from:

the state superintendent of public instruction, as provided in Subsection 53E-3-706(3), if the school district, charter school, or micro-education entity used an independent building inspector for inspection of the school building; or
a county official with authority to issue the certificate, if the school district, charter school, or micro-education entity used a county building inspector for inspection of the school building.

A school district may issue its own certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building if it used its own building inspector for inspection of the school building, subject to the notification requirement of Subsection 53E-3-706(3)(d)(ii).

A charter school or micro-education entity may seek a certificate authorizing permanent occupancy of a school building from a school district official with authority to issue the certificate, if the charter school or micro-education entity used a school district building inspector for inspection of the school building.

A certificate authorizing permanent occupancy issued by the state superintendent of public instruction under Subsection 53E-3-706(3) or a school district official with authority to issue the certificate shall be considered to satisfy any county requirement for an inspection or a certificate of occupancy.

subsection.subsection: (i)A micro-education entity may operate a facility that meets Group E Occupancy requirements as defined by the International Building Code, as incorporated by Subsection 15A-2-103(1)(a).
(ii)A micro-education entity operating in a facility described in Subsection (7)(f)(i) may have up to 100 students in the facility.

(g)A micro-education entity may operate a facility that is subject to and complies with the same occupancy requirements as a Class A-1, A-3, B, or M Occupancy as defined by the International Building Code, as incorporated by Subsection 15A-2-103(1)(a), if:(i)the facility has a code compliant fire alarm system and carbon monoxide detection system;(ii)(A)each classroom in the facility has an exit directly to the outside at the level of exit discharge; or(B)the structure has a code compliant fire sprinkler system; and(iii)the facility has an automatic fire sprinkler system in fire areas of the facility that are greater than 12,000 square feet.
(i)A home-based microschool is not subject to additional occupancy requirements beyond occupancy requirements that apply to a primary dwelling.

(ii)If a floor that is below grade in a home-based microschool is used for home-based microschool purposes, the below grade floor of the home-based microschool shall have at least one emergency escape or rescue window that complies with the requirements for emergency escape and rescue windows as defined by the International Residential Code, as incorporated in Section 15A-1-210.

(a)A specified public agency intending to develop its land shall submit to the land use authority a development plan and schedule:(i)as early as practicable in the development process, but no later than the commencement of construction; and(ii)with sufficient detail to enable the land use authority to assess:(A)the specified public agency's compliance with applicable land use ordinances;(B)the demand for public facilities listed in Subsections 11-36a-102(17)(a), (b), (c), (d), (e), and (g) caused by the development;(C)the amount of any applicable fee described in Section 17-79-804;(D)any credit against an impact fee; and(E)the potential for waiving an impact fee.

(b)The land use authority shall respond to a specified public agency's submission under Subsection (8)(a) with reasonable promptness in order to allow the specified public agency to consider information the municipality provides under Subsection (8)(a)(ii) in the process of preparing the budget for the development.

section.subsection: (9)Nothing in this section may be construed to:(a)modify or supersede Section 17-79-306; or(b)authorize a county to enforce an ordinance in a way, or enact an ordinance, that fails to comply with Title 57, Chapter 21, Utah Fair Housing Act, the federal Fair Housing Amendments Act of 1988, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 3601 et seq., the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 12102, or any other provision of federal law.

(10)Nothing in Subsection (7) prevents a political subdivision from:(a)requiring a home-based microschool or micro-education entity to comply with local zoning and land use regulations that do not conflict with this section, including:(i)parking;(ii)traffic; and(iii)hours of operation;(b)requiring a home-based microschool or micro-education entity to obtain a business license;(c)enacting county ordinances and regulations consistent with this section;(d)subjecting a micro-education entity to standards within each zone pertaining to setback, height, bulk and massing regulations, off-site parking, curb cut, traffic circulation, and construction staging; and(e)imposing regulations on the location of a project that are necessary to avoid risks to health or safety.
(11)Notwithstanding any other provision of law, the proximity restrictions that apply to community locations do not apply to a micro-education entity.

subsection.amend: Section 63A-5b-1218 governs the requirements for permitting and inspection of public school buildings.
A county may not enact any ordinance, policy, or regulation relating to the permitting or inspection of public school buildings.
Nothing in this section shall prohibit the required regulation or subsequent inspection by a local health department.

section.secline: Section 3. Section 53E-3-701 is repealed and reenacted to read:

sectionText.amend: As used in this part, "division" means the same as that term is defined in Section 63A-5b-102.

section.secline: Section 4. Section 53E-3-702 is repealed and reenacted to read:

catline.bold: 53E-3-702. State board coordination with Division of Facilities Construction and Management.

subsection.amend: The state board shall coordinate with the division regarding public school construction oversight as described in Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight, through the school construction liaison designated under Section 63A-5b-1212.
In providing the coordination described in Subsection (1), the state board may:
provide input on educational programming requirements that affect construction standards;
participate in construction rules development and review;
assist with training programs for school district officials;
coordinate on matters affecting educational programs and construction oversight; and
maintain expertise in educational facility planning and design.
The state board shall maintain the state board's authority over:
educational program requirements that may affect facility needs;
coordination between construction projects and educational planning;
policies related to educational use of facilities; and
other matters not specifically described in Title 63A, Chapter 5b, Part 12, Public School Construction Oversight.

section.secline: Section 5. Section 53E-3-703 is repealed and reenacted to read:

subsection.amend: In planning school construction, a school district shall consider:
educational program requirements for different grade levels;
year-round use requirements;
specific needs of various student populations; and
coordination between facility design and educational delivery.

A school district shall ensure a licensed architect prepares plans and specifications for school construction or alteration, subject to cost oversight requirements established by the division described in Section 63A-5b-1204.
A school district shall ensure all construction contracts include the provisions for construction contracts as the division determines.

section.secline: Section 6. Section 53E-3-704 is repealed and reenacted to read:

subsection.amend: A school district shall comply with Title 63G, Chapter 6a, Utah Procurement Code, for all construction contracts.
A school district shall require payment and performance bonds as provided in Section 14-1-18 for construction contracts exceeding $50,000.
A school district shall ensure a construction contract includes:
contract provisions the division establishes under Subsection 53E-3-703(6);
insurance requirements meeting division standards; and
dispute resolution procedures.

section.secline: Section 7. Section 63A-5b-1201 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: This part does not apply to micro-education entities as defined in Section 53G-6-201.
As used in this part:

"Public school construction" means construction work on a new public school facility or major alteration of an existing public school facility with a total project cost exceeding the delineated thresholds for a capital development project or capital improvement project as defined in Section 63A-5b-401.
"School construction liaison" means the individual designated under Section 63A-5b-1212 to coordinate between the division and the state board regarding public school construction matters.
"School district" means a local school district established under Title 53G, Chapter 3, School District Creation and Change.
"State board" means the same as that term is defined in Section 53E-1-102.

section.secline: Section 8. Section 63A-5b-1202 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 63A-5b-1202. Division authority over public school construction -- Construction standards.

subsection.amend: In accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, the division shall make rules establishing comprehensive standards for public school construction that shall include:
minimum and maximum real property requirements;
cost per square foot parameters;
building material quality and cost standards;
parking and access requirements;
standardized cost reporting requirements;
mandatory plan review and approval procedures;
qualified reviewer certification requirements;
a process for permitting of public school construction in accordance with Section 63A-5b-1218;
a process for a school district to report project related issues;
other construction related requirements;
transition and replacement of existing State Board of Education rules related to school construction;
facility condition assessment requirements, including:
frequency of assessments;
qualifications for third-party engineering firms conducting assessments;
standardized assessment methodologies; and
reporting and database requirements;
building maintenance standards for public school facilities;
building commissioning requirements, including:
commissioning scope and procedures;
qualified commissioning agent requirements;
functional performance testing standards;
commissioning documentation and closeout requirements; and
ongoing commissioning for major building systems;
construction delivery method criteria and approval procedures consistent with Section 63G-6a-1302;
construction manager or general contractor use restrictions and oversight requirements in accordance with Subsections 63G-6a-1302(4) and (5);
factors to be considered in selecting appropriate delivery methods under Subsection 63G-6a-1302(3);
standards for school district coordination with municipalities and counties regarding school siting, traffic impacts, and site safety considerations; and
cost matrix establishment and application, including:
cost per square foot parameters by building type and region;
allowable cost ranges and variance thresholds;
factors for adjusting costs based on project complexity;
pre-construction approval procedures and timelines; and
appeal procedures for cost matrix determinations.
In developing the rules described in Subsection (1), the division shall consider:
project costs;
location factors, including rural or urban setting and climate;
projected student population growth or declines;
specific needs of different school types and grade levels;
year-round use requirements; and
accessibility compliance under state and federal law.
The division shall consult with:
the fire marshal;
the school construction liaison on educational programming needs and coordination of safety and security requirements;
representatives from school districts; and
architects, engineers, and construction professionals.
As described in Section 63A-5b-1213, the division may establish fee-for-service construction management programs to:
provide professional construction oversight for a school district lacking internal capacity;
ensure consistent application of standards across all projects;
leverage state procurement expertise and economies of scale; and
reduce compliance risks for complex projects.
The division shall require a school district to:
review division rules and policies before beginning construction;
demonstrate compliance with rules and policies as a condition of permit approval;
use qualified, certified inspectors for all required inspections; and
submit monthly construction reports through the online system described in Section 63A-5b-1207.
The school construction liaison, in coordination with the School Safety Center established under Section 53G-8-802 and the state security chief appointed under Section 53-22-102, shall:
ensure compliance with mandatory safety and security standards established under Section 53-22-102;
review construction plans to verify that proposed designs meet mandatory safety and security requirements;
coordinate with the division regarding safety and security compliance before the division issues construction permits; and
report safety and security compliance to the division as part of the plan approval process under Section 63A-5b-1205.
The division shall collaborate with the state fire marshal, state security chief, and local governmental entities to:
examine plans and specifications for school buildings;
verify inspections during and following construction; and
perform other functions necessary to ensure compliance.
In exercising oversight authority, the division shall recognize that:
public school construction is typically funded through local bond measures approved by school district voters;
school facilities are owned and operated by the school district;
local communities have legitimate interests in facility design and construction decisions; and
division oversight is intended to ensure standardized cost controls, compliance with safety, efficiency, and accountability standards while preserving appropriate local autonomy.
The division may delegate oversight authority to qualified school districts as provided in Section 63A-5b-1217.
A school district may appeal division determinations under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.
The division shall require:
building commissioning for all new construction and major renovations exceeding $5,000,000, including:
commissioning of mechanical, electrical, plumbing, and building envelope systems;
verification of system performance against design intent;
training of facility staff on commissioned systems; and
delivery of comprehensive commissioning documentation;
facility condition assessments:
every five years for all facilities over 20 years old;
conducted by qualified third-party architectural and engineering firms;
addressing all major building systems and components; and
integrated with the deferred maintenance reporting under Section 63A-5b-1210.

section.secline: Section 9. Section 63A-5b-1203 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: For public school construction exceeding $3,000,000, a school district shall use one of the following delivery methods authorized under Section 63G-6a-1302:
design-bid-build;
design-build; or
a construction manager general contractor, subject to the restrictions in Subsection (2).
A school district may use a construction manager general contractor delivery method if:
the school district demonstrates to the division that the project meets appropriate criteria for using a construction manager general contractor under Subsection 63G-6a-1302(3);
the school district employs or contracts with qualified construction management staff with demonstrated construction manager general contractor experience; and
the school district submits a detailed project management plan approved by the division;
The division may only deny approval for a construction manager general contractor delivery method if:
the school district lacks sufficient expertise to manage the project;
the project does not meet appropriate criteria under Subsection 63G-6a-1302(3); or
other methods of delivery would provide better cost control and project outcomes.
The division shall:
establish clear criteria for when a construction manager general contractor delivery method is appropriate, considering the factors described in Subsection 63G-6a-1302(3);
provide training to a school district on:
proper implementation of each delivery method authorized under Section 63G-6a-1302;
cost control measures for each delivery method; and
circumstances when each delivery method is most appropriate; and
require additional oversight and reporting for projects using a construction manager or general contractor.
For projects under $3,000,000, a school district shall use design-bid-build unless the division approves an alternative delivery method under Section 63G-6a-1302.
A school district shall comply with all procurement requirements under Title 63G, Chapter 6a, Utah Procurement Code, when using any construction delivery method, including:
requirements for selection of contractors in Subsection 63G-6a-1302(5) when using a construction manager or general contractor;
requirements for subcontractor procurement in Subsection 63G-6a-1302(5)(b); and
any other applicable procurement standards established in Title 63G, Chapter 6a, Utah Procurement Code.
For projects exceeding $500,000, a school district shall select contractors only from the qualified contractor registry established under Section 63A-5b-1216.
The contractor registry requirement in Subsection (6):
establishes pre-qualification standards but does not replace competitive procurement requirements;
limits the pool of eligible bidders to qualified contractors; and
applies to all delivery methods authorized under this section.

section.secline: Section 10. Section 63A-5b-1204 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 63A-5b-1204. Architect and design professional cost oversight.

subsection.amend: The division shall establish and publish maximum allowable fees for both basic services and complexities for architect and design professional services based on:
project size and complexity;
regional market conditions;
industry standards for educational facilities; and
historical cost data from comparable projects.
A school district shall:
ensure that no contract for architect or design professional services exceeds the maximum fees established by the division under Subsection (1);
submit all proposed contracts to the division for verification of compliance with maximum fee limits before execution; and
demonstrate through competitive procurement that proposed fees represent fair market value within the established maximums.
If a school district believes a project requires fees exceeding the division's maximum allowable fees, the school district shall:
submit a detailed written request to the division before soliciting proposals;
provide documentation of unique project circumstances requiring specialized expertise;
demonstrate that the project cannot be completed within the fee schedule; and
obtain written approval from the division before proceeding.
The division shall:
review cost submissions within 15 business days;
provide written explanation for any required modifications; and
update cost benchmarks annually based on market conditions.
The division shall develop and maintain a library of standardized school design templates developed in collaboration with architects experienced in the design of school facilities that:
provide pre-approved design options for common school types and sizes;
reduce architectural costs through standardized specifications;
accelerate the approval process for school districts using standard designs;
allow modifications for site-specific requirements; and
are updated every five years to reflect current educational needs.
The division shall develop and maintain a library of standardized school design prototypes developed in collaboration with architects experienced in the design of school facilities that:
provide pre-approved design options for common school types and sizes;
reduce architectural costs through:
reduced design scope for projects using prototype designs;
standardized specifications and details;
elimination of redundant design work; and
lower fee schedule tiers for prototype-based projects;
accelerate the approval process through:
pre-approved building systems and layouts;
reduced plan review time for projects adhering to prototype designs; and
streamlined permitting when no significant prototype modifications are made;
allow modifications for site-specific requirements; and
are updated every five years to reflect current educational needs and building standards.
A school district using a standardized design prototype:
shall comply with all procurement requirements under Title 63G, Chapter 6a, Utah Procurement Code;
may receive reduced architect fees reflecting the limited scope of site adaptation work;
may receive an expedited plan review within 10 business days if no significant modifications are made;
is encouraged to use design-build delivery methods with prototype designs to maximize cost savings; and
shall coordinate with the division on any proposed modifications to ensure continued compliance with prototype standards.
A school district shall procure architect and design professional services:
in accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 6a, Part 15, Design Professional Services;
using qualifications-based selection procedures as required by Subsection 63G-6a-1502(1);
based on demonstrated competence and qualification for the type of services required; and
not based solely on price.
A school district may appeal the division's maximum fee determinations or denials of requests for fee exceptions to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.

section.secline: Section 11. Section 63A-5b-1205 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: In accordance with the division's permitting authority described in Section 63A-5b-1218, the division shall review and approve or deny all construction documents for public school construction:
within 10 business days; and
before a school district may begin construction.
The division's plan review shall verify compliance with:
adopted building codes and standards;
division construction rules;
accessibility requirements;
energy code compliance;
structural engineering requirements;
fire and school safety provisions;
mandatory safety and security standards;
emergency response and evacuation protocols required under Subsection 53-22-102(3)(c); and
other building or construction codes the division determines relevant.
The division may require corrections to construction documents and may not approve plans that fail to meet applicable standards.
A school district may not begin construction until the division has issued a written approval of the construction documents and a permit under Section 63A-5b-1218.
Before approving any construction contract exceeding $1,000,000, a school district shall:
submit proposed contract costs to the division for benchmark comparison when such comparisons become reasonably available following the division's establishment of a baseline;
provide written justification for any costs exceeding division benchmarks by more than 10%;
demonstrate compliance with standardized procurement procedures; and
receive division approval before contract execution.
Before beginning any public school construction project, a school district shall:
submit a pre-construction application to the division that includes:
detailed project scope and specifications;
preliminary cost estimates;
a proposed project timeline; and
funding sources and financial capacity documentation;
receive division approval that the project:
fits within the cost matrix established by division rule;
meets cost per square foot parameters for the building type and region;
demonstrates reasonable and appropriate costs for the proposed scope; and
complies with all applicable standards and requirements; and
demonstrate that the project costs are consistent with:
division cost benchmarks for similar projects;
regional cost factors;
current market conditions; and
industry standards for educational facilities.
The division shall:
review pre-construction applications within 15 business days;
approve, conditionally approve with modifications, or deny applications based on cost matrix compliance;
provide written explanation for any required modifications or denials;
work with the school district to bring non-compliant projects into compliance with the cost matrix; and
maintain records of all project approvals and cost matrix determinations.
The division may not issue a construction permit under Section 63A-5b-1218 until the project has received cost matrix approval under this section.
The division may delegate plan review responsibilities to qualified third-party reviewers subject to division oversight and final approval.
A school district may appeal the following determinations under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221:
denial of construction permit applications;
cost matrix determinations under Subsections (6) and (7);
required plan corrections under Subsection (3);
pre-construction application denials under Subsection (7)(b); and
conditions imposed on project approvals.

section.secline: Section 12. Section 63A-5b-1206 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division shall establish and enforce specific technical standards including:
fire wall construction and continuity requirements as described in International Building Code, Chapter 7;
accessibility compliance verification procedures as described in International Code Council A117.1;
energy code inspection protocols with mandatory R-value verification;
mechanical, plumbing, and electrical system review depth requirements;
special inspection requirements for both structural and nonstructural elements; and
structural observation reporting mandates for Risk Category III buildings as as defined in the International Building Code adopted under Section 15A-1-204.
The division may not issue a construction permit without verified compliance with Subsection (1).
The division shall use qualified plan reviewers and inspectors from the roster established under Section 63A-5b-1220 for all plan review and inspection activities.
Requirements related to safety and security standards established under Section 53-22-102 shall be coordinated through the school construction liaison as provided in Subsection 63A-5b-1202(6).
A school district may appeal technical compliance determinations under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.

section.secline: Section 13. Section 63A-5b-1207 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division shall develop and maintain an online document management system that:
maintains all required construction and inspection documentation;
enables real-time verification of compliance with statutes and rules;
tracks pre-construction permit submissions and approvals;
facilitates enforcement through automated notifications;
provides reporting capabilities for oversight and auditing; and
ensures secure access for authorized users.
A school district shall use the online system to:
submit all required pre-construction documentation;
upload inspection reports and certifications;
report construction costs using standardized formats; and
track project milestones and compliance status.
The division shall provide training and technical support for school districts, including proper use of the online document management system.

section.secline: Section 14. Section 63A-5b-1208 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division shall establish inspection verification procedures that:
go beyond simple attestation to verify actual compliance;
include regular monitoring of inspection documentation;
provide mechanisms for addressing noncompliance; and
coordinate with local governmental entities as required by law, including Sections 10-9a-305 and 17-27a-305.
Before beginning construction, a school district shall:
provide all required notices in accordance with Section 53E-3-703;
obtain all required permits from the division in accordance with Section 63A-5b-1218;
submit complete construction documents to the division;
demonstrate compliance with division rules and policies; and
provide evidence of qualified inspection arrangements that meet the requirements described in Subsection 63A-5b-1220.
During construction, a school district shall:
use qualified, certified inspectors the division approves for all required inspections;
submit inspection reports through the online system described in Section 63A-5b-1207;
notify the division of any significant changes or issues;
maintain detailed inspection records;
coordinate with local building officials;
as required, provide special inspection reports for all International Building Code, Chapter 17, requirements;
submit structural observation reports for buildings with an occupancy greater than 250; and
document correction of all deficiencies before proceeding.
The division may:
conduct random audits of inspection procedures;
require additional inspections when warranted;
review and approve alternative inspection arrangements; and
suspend construction permits for serious violations.
Upon completion of construction and verification of all required inspections, the division shall issue a certificate authorizing permanent occupancy if:
all required inspections have been completed by qualified inspectors on the division's approved roster;
all inspection reports verify compliance with applicable codes and standards;
all identified deficiencies have been corrected and documented;
the school district has submitted all required closeout documentation through the online system described in Section 63A-5b-1207;
structural observation reports have been submitted for buildings with an occupancy greater than 250 as required under Subsection (3)(g);
special inspection reports verify compliance with all International Building Code, Chapter 17, requirements; and
the project complies with mandatory safety and security standards established by the state security chief under Section 53-22-102.
The division shall:
review occupancy certificate applications within 10 business days of receiving complete documentation;
issue written certificates that include:
the project address and description;
the authorized occupancy type and capacity;
verification that all inspections confirm code compliance;
the effective date of the certificate; and
any conditions or limitations on occupancy;
maintain a database of all occupancy certificates issued; and
provide copies of certificates to:
the school district;
the municipality or county where the facility is located; and
the local fire authority.
A certificate authorizing permanent occupancy issued by the division under this section:
is the exclusive certificate required for public school facilities subject to this part;
satisfies all municipal and county occupancy requirements under Sections 10-20-304 and 17-79-305; and
authorizes the school district to occupy and use the facility for educational purposes.
Municipalities and counties shall accept the division's certificate without requiring additional local certificates of occupancy.
If the division determines that a facility does not meet requirements for occupancy, the division shall:
deny the occupancy certificate application;
provide written explanation of all deficiencies requiring correction;
specify a timeline for the school district to address deficiencies; and
allow the school district to resubmit for occupancy certification after corrections are made.
The school district shall notify the division within 10 days of beginning occupancy of any facility for which the division has issued an occupancy certificate.
For temporary or partial occupancy during construction:
a school district may request a temporary certificate of occupancy from the division;
the division may issue a temporary certificate if:
the portion to be occupied meets all safety and code requirements;
the school district demonstrates that temporary occupancy will not create safety hazards;
appropriate separation exists between occupied and construction areas; and
the school district provides a timeline for completing remaining work; and
temporary certificates expire upon the earlier of:
issuance of a permanent occupancy certificate; or
180 days from issuance, unless extended by the division for good cause.
A school district may appeal the following determinations under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221:
denial of occupancy certificates under Subsection (8);
conditions or limitations imposed on occupancy under Subsection (6)(b)(v);
denial of temporary occupancy certificates under Subsection (10);
requirements for additional inspections under Subsection (4)(b); and
determinations that deficiencies shall be corrected before occupancy.

section.secline: Section 15. Section 63A-5b-1209 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 63A-5b-1209. Historical project documentation and information requests.

subsection.amend: Upon written request from the division, a school district shall provide:
documentation and information regarding past construction projects, including projects completed before the effective date of this part;
cost data, construction documents, and project records;
contractor and vendor information and performance records;
change order documentation and justifications;
inspection records and certificates of occupancy;
as-built drawings and project closeout documentation;
warranty information and maintenance records; and
any other project-related information the division determines necessary for:
establishing cost benchmarks and databases;
analyzing construction cost trends;
developing standardized design templates;
creating training programs and best practices; or
other purposes related to public school construction oversight.
A school district shall provide requested information:
within 90 days of receiving the division's written request;
in the format specified by the division;
organized and indexed for efficient review;
with complete documentation for each requested project; and
at no cost to the division.
If a school district is unable to provide requested information within 30 days due to volume or complexity, the school district shall:
notify the division within 10 days of receiving the request;
provide a detailed explanation of the delay;
propose a reasonable timeline for providing the information; and
provide interim updates on progress.
The division may use the information collected under this section to:
populate the construction cost database described in Section 63A-5b-1210;
establish baseline cost benchmarks;
identify best practices and areas for improvement;
develop training materials and resources;
create standardized design templates;
conduct research on construction cost drivers; and
provide better technical assistance to a school district.
The division shall:
maintain the confidentiality of proprietary information submitted by a school district;
use submitted information only for the purposes described in this section;
aggregate data when publishing reports or benchmarks to protect school district-specific information where appropriate; and
provide a school district with access to aggregated data and analysis.
Failure of a school district to provide requested information without good cause may result in:
loss of certain oversight autonomy, including requirement for division-managed construction services under Section 63A-5b-1213; or
administrative penalties not exceeding $5,000, subject to appeal under Section 63A-5b-1221.
This section applies to:
all school districts;
projects completed within the last 5 years, including projects completed before January 1, 2027; and
both new construction and major renovation projects.

section.secline: Section 16. Section 63A-5b-1210 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division shall implement a standardized cost reporting system that:
requires a school district to report construction costs using consistent categories and formats;
enables accurate cost comparison between projects and districts;
tracks cost trends and identifies potential cost savings;
provides data for legislative oversight and budget planning; and
contains detailed cost reporting requirements, including:
pre-construction cost estimates using the Construction Specifications Institute MasterFormat categories;
monthly cost reports during construction with variance explanations;
change order documentation with justification and approval authority;
final reconciled costs with cost-per-square-foot analysis;
separate reporting of construction, equipment, and soft costs;
comparison to division cost benchmarks by building type and region;
supports for audit and compliance activities; and
annual deferred maintenance reporting including:
identification of all maintenance items deferred during the reporting period;
estimated costs for each deferred maintenance item;
priority ranking based on safety, functionality, and cost implications;
projected timeline for addressing deferred items; and
cumulative deferred maintenance backlog by facility and system type.
Notwithstanding the division's tracking of deferred maintenance, the division and the state assume no liability for any deferred maintenance costs or issues arising from deferred maintenance.
The school district shall retain all liability for deferred maintenance costs and any consequences resulting from deferred maintenance decisions.
The division shall:
analyze cost data to identify trends and outliers;
provide cost guidance to a school district;
upon request, report to the Legislature on construction cost trends; and
make aggregated cost data available to support planning and budgeting.
The division shall establish and maintain a comprehensive construction cost database that:
is accessible to the State Board of Education and other relevant stakeholders as the division determines;
consolidates historical cost data from all school district projects completed after January 1, 2025;
provides cost benchmarks updated quarterly;
identifies cost outliers and investigates variances exceeding 15%;
generates annual cost trend reports for legislative review;
supports a school district budget planning with predictive cost modeling;
tracks deferred maintenance trends across a school district and facility types;
identifies facilities with excessive deferred maintenance backlogs requiring intervention; and
provides predictive modeling for long-term maintenance and replacement costs.
Beginning July 1, 2029, a school district shall submit annual deferred maintenance reports to the division that:
identify all maintenance items with estimated costs exceeding $25,000 that were deferred during the fiscal year, limited to items described in Subsections 63A-5b-405(2)(c)(i) and (ii);
provide justification for deferral decisions;
include facility condition assessments updated at least every five years;
demonstrate how deferred maintenance decisions align with long-term capital planning; and
report on progress addressing previously identified deferred maintenance items.
The division shall implement the requirements of this section in phases as follows:
by July 1, 2027:
complete initial cost database framework development;
begin collecting historical cost data under Section 63A-5b-1209; and
develop standardized reporting formats;
by July 1, 2028:
establish preliminary cost benchmarks for elementary and secondary schools;
implement basic cost comparison tools; and
provide initial training to school districts on standardized cost reporting;
by July 1, 2029:
complete baseline cost benchmarks for all major building types and regions;
implement automated cost comparison and analysis tools; and
establish full compliance requirements for deferred maintenance reporting; and
by July 1, 2030:
achieve full implementation of all cost database and reporting requirements; and
provide comprehensive training and technical assistance to all school districts.
Beginning December 1, 2027, the division shall report annually to:
the Education Interim Committee; and
the Transportation and Infrastructure Appropriations Subcommittee.
The annual report shall include:
progress on phased implementation under Subsection (6);
status of cost database development and benchmark establishment;
implementation status of automated cost comparison tools;
training provided to school districts on standardized cost reporting;
analysis of construction cost trends and outliers;
deferred maintenance trends across a school district and facility types;
compliance audit results and enforcement actions taken;
construction oversight fee revenue and expenditures; and
recommendations for statutory or policy improvements.

section.secline: Section 17. Section 63A-5b-1211 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: In collaboration with the school construction liaison described in Section 63A-5b-1212, the division shall develop and maintain a school construction resource manual including:
current legal requirements;
construction and inspection rules;
compliance procedures and documentation standards; and
standardized forms and reporting requirements.
The division shall review and update the manual at least every three years or when significant changes in building codes occur.
In collaboration with the school construction liaison described in Section 63A-5b-1212, the division shall provide annual training for school district officials covering:
construction oversight responsibilities and procedures;
building code compliance requirements;
inspection documentation and verification;
cost reporting procedures;
use of the online management system described in Section 63A-5b-1207; and
coordination with local building officials.
The division may not use a third-party vendor or provider to fulfill the requirements of Subsection (3)(a).
The division shall provide additional training for:
design professionals working on school projects;
third-party plan reviewers and inspectors;
construction managers and general contractors; and
building officials involved in school construction as the division determines.
The division shall update trainings regularly to reflect:
changes in building codes and standards;
new construction technologies and methods;
lessons learned from project audits and reviews; and
best practices from other jurisdictions.
The division may:
charge reasonable fees for specialized training programs; and
in accordance with Subsection (3)(b), contract with qualified entities to provide training services.

section.secline: Section 18. Section 63A-5b-1212 is enacted to read:

catline.bold: 63A-5b-1212. School construction liaison -- Coordination with state board.

subsection.amend: The state board shall designate a school construction liaison who:
serves as the primary contact between the state board and the division on school construction matters;
coordinates on educational programming requirements that may affect construction standards;
participates in administrative rule development and review processes;
provides input on educational facility needs and trends;
assists with training programs for school district officials; and
facilitates resolution of construction-related issues affecting educational programs.
The school construction liaison shall:
be an employee of the state board;
have expertise in school construction, educational facilities, or related fields;
participate in relevant division meetings and committees;
maintain regular communication with division staff; and
report to the state board on construction oversight activities and issues.
The division and state board shall establish a memorandum of understanding that:
defines roles and responsibilities for school construction oversight;
establishes communication protocols and meeting schedules;
addresses coordination on policy development and implementation;
provides for information sharing and joint training activities; and
includes dispute resolution procedures.

section.secline: Section 19. Section 63A-5b-1213 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division may provide comprehensive construction management services to a school district, including:
project procurement and contractor selection;
construction oversight and inspection coordination;
cost management and change order approval;
compliance verification and quality assurance;
project scheduling and milestone management; and
architect and design professional services when required under Section 63A-5b-1204 or requested by a school district.
A school district may request division construction management services for:
any project exceeding $3,500,000 in total cost;
complex projects requiring specialized expertise; or
projects where the school district lacks sufficient internal capacity.
The division shall provide construction management services when:
requested by a school district and capacity allows; or
a school district has demonstrated repeated compliance failures.
The division shall establish a cost structure for managed services where:
the school district reimburses the division for all direct project costs;
administrative fees do not exceed:
3% of total project cost; and
for projects under $3,500,000, the actual cost of providing management services in relation to the specific project;
fee schedules are published annually in division rules; and
cost savings achieved through division management may be shared with the school district.
The division shall ensure:
the administrative fee structure for division-managed projects includes the construction oversight fee required by Section 63A-5b-1214; and

amend.marker: a school district may not pay duplicative fees.

subsection.amend: The division shall provide a school district with a detailed fee breakdown showing:
additional direct project management costs; and
the total administrative fee percentage.
The division shall ensure that for division-managed projects:
the division assumes full responsibility for code compliance;
all procurement follows state procurement rules;
the school district retains authority over educational program requirements; and
regular progress and financial reports are provided to the school district governing board as defined in Section 53E-1-102.
A school district may appeal the division's determination to require division-managed construction services to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.

section.secline: Section 20. Section 63A-5b-1214 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: Except for a school district that has hired a division approved project manager to provide services as described in this section, for all public school construction projects, a school district shall pay the division a construction oversight, building official, plan review, and administration fee in accordance with a fee schedule established by division rule under Section 63A-5b-1217 and approved by the rate committee created in Section 63A-1-114.
The construction oversight fee:
applies to all projects subject to this part, regardless of whether the division is providing direct construction management services;
shall be calculated based on the total project cost including:
construction contracts;
architect and design professional fees;
furniture, fixtures, and equipment;
site development and utility connections;
testing and inspection services; and
other project-related costs;

shall be established by division rule under Section 63A-5b-1217 to recover the reasonable costs of oversight activities and may be reduced or waived for school districts with delegated oversight authority; and
may be included in the school district's bond authorization or other funding mechanism.
The construction oversight fee assessed to a school district shall fund the division's activities, including:
plan review and permitting;
inspection roster maintenance and oversight;
cost database development and maintenance;
training and technical assistance programs;
compliance monitoring and enforcement;
online document management system operation;
standardized design template development; and
other oversight activities required by this part.
For projects where the division provides direct construction management services under Section 63A-5b-1213:
the school district shall not pay duplicative fees; and
the division shall clearly delineate the fee structure for oversight activities.
The division shall:
deposit all construction oversight fees into the restricted account established in Section 63A-5b-1219;
use fee revenue exclusively for public school construction oversight activities;
report annually to the Legislature on fee revenue and expenditures; and
adjust the fee rate only through legislative action.
A school district may appeal fee assessments or payment schedule requirements under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.

section.secline: Section 21. Section 63A-5b-1215 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: If a school district fails to comply with requirements established under this part, the division may:
issue written warnings and compliance orders;
suspend or revoke construction permits;
require additional inspections at the school district's expense;
impose administrative penalties as provided in rule; and
refer matters to appropriate authorities for further action.
The division may not assess administrative penalties that exceed:
$1,000 for minor violations;
$5,000 for significant violations; and
$25,000 for violations that pose safety risks or involve willful noncompliance.
Before imposing penalties, the division shall:
provide written notice of alleged violations;
allow reasonable time for correction;
provide opportunity for a hearing; and
consider the school district's compliance history and efforts to remedy violations.
Penalty funds collected shall be deposited into the account established in Section 63A-5b-1219.
A school district may appeal enforcement actions, penalties, or other division decisions under this section to the appeals panel established under Section 63A-5b-1221.
The division shall conduct mandatory compliance audits of:
10% of all projects annually, selected randomly;
any project exceeding cost benchmarks by more than 20%;
any school district with previous compliance violations; and
any project with reported safety concerns.
For serious violations involving life safety, the division may:
require immediate work stoppage until corrections are made;
mandate third-party oversight at a school district's expense;
require remedial training for school district officials; and
refer matters to the applicable professional licensing board.

section.secline: Section 22. Section 63A-5b-1216 is enacted to read:

subsection.amend: The division shall establish and maintain a pre-qualification registry of contractors and vendors eligible to bid on public school construction projects as an original contractor, as that term is defined in Section 38-1a-102, that:
establishes minimum qualification standards based on experience, financial capacity, and performance history;
standardizes contractor evaluation criteria across all school districts;
provides performance ratings based on completed projects;
enables volume purchasing agreements for common materials and services; and
facilitates cost comparison across contractors and regions.
To be eligible to bid on public school construction exceeding $500,000, a contractor shall:
register with the division's contractor registry;
meet minimum qualifications established by division rule;
maintain current insurance and licensing requirements;
submit to performance evaluations on completed projects; and
participate in division-sponsored training programs.
